editor_settings.title: I Tried the Scotty Cameron Circle T GoLo 6.5 So You Don’t Have To
editor_settings.content:
In the world of high-stakes golf, the name Scotty Cameron carries a weight that few other brands can match. It is the Rolex of putters—highly engineered, aesthetically flawless, and surrounded by a level of hype that can make even a rational golfer consider spending mortgage money on a piece of milled steel.

This 34-inch left-handed Select GOLO 6.5 (TG6.5) Tourtype SSS Prototype is coming soon to Scotty's online gallery with a welded jet neck that includes a pink Champs Choice Circle T mid-mallet headcover and a Tour Only Full Contact grip. pic.twitter.com/qpYKVLwMfu

— Scotty Cameron (@ScottyCameron) May 7, 2025

Quick Verdict

The Scotty Cameron Circle T GoLo 6.5 is an absolute precision instrument for a specific type of player, but for 95% of golfers, it is a luxury flex rather than a performance necessity. It is a world-class flatstick, but the "Circle T" premium is paid for in prestige, not just strokes saved.

Who It’s For / Who Should Skip

  • Buy it if: You have a distinct arcing stroke, you value top-tier collectible assets, and you want the exact equipment used by the world's best professionals.

  • Skip it if: you struggle with a straight-back, straight-through stroke or if you expect a $4,000 putter to magically fix a fundamental alignment issue.

Design & First Impressions

The GoLo 6.5 sits in that "compact mallet" sweet spot. It doesn't look like a spaceship, but it offers more stability than a traditional blade. The Circle T versions, particularly the Tourtype SSS Prototypes, feature a level of hand-finishing that you simply won't find on a mass-produced rack at a big-box retailer. The "SSS" (Studio Stainless Steel) provides a crisp yet soft feedback that is the benchmark for the industry.

Specs That Actually Matter

  • I-Beam Jet Neck: This is the secret sauce for the 6.5. It promotes "mid-high" toe flow, giving mallet stability to players who prefer the feel of a blade.

  • Dual-Milled Face: A second pass of the mill levels the face grooves, creating a more consistent roll and a "Goldilocks" feel—neither too mushy nor too clicky.

  • 303 Stainless Steel: The standard for durability and vibration dampening.

Real-World Performance

On the green, the GoLo 6.5 shines for the player who likes to feel the putter head rotating through the arc. The integrated 6061 aircraft aluminum sole plate allows weight to be pushed to the perimeter, increasing the Moment of Inertia (MOI). This means it is more forgiving on off-center hits than a Newport, but it still requires a steady hand.


The Hype Factor

The "Circle T" logo—signifying a putter "For Tour Use Only"—is the ultimate status symbol in golf. Because these aren't sold in standard retail stores, scarcity drives the market. Social media accounts and "Gallery" drops have turned these into investment pieces as much as sports equipment.

@peoplesgolf 🔥 @Scotty Cameron #CircleT TG6.5 in tour black featuring a welded flojet neck and black sole plate only available on tour. #onlythebest #tourgolo #tg6 #PeoplesGolf ♬ original sound - mega


Pricing Reality Check

A standard retail Super Select GoLo 6.5 will cost you around $450. However, once you step into "Circle T" territory, the floor starts at $2,500 and can easily climb to $8,000 or more for prototypes with unique welded necks or exotic finishes.

If you look beyond the obvious high-end auction sites, you can find used Tour-issued models for closer to $1,800 to $2,200. While that sounds steep, these putters often appreciate in value. It is essentially "free" to own if you buy it at a fair price and take care of it, as you can almost always sell it for what you paid—or more.



Comparison Section

  • Scotty Cameron Super Select (Retail): 90% of the performance for 15% of the price. Best for 99% of golfers.

  • Bettinardi BB Series: Excellent milling and similar feel, often at a more "reasonable" luxury price point, though without the same resale cult following.

  • L.A.B. Golf Mezz.1: If you want pure technology and "zero torque" performance over traditional aesthetics, this is the functional alternative.

Is It Worth It?

If you are a collector or a scratch golfer who demands the absolute pinnacle of feel and "toe flow" in a mallet, the Circle T GoLo 6.5 is the peak. If you are just looking to shave three putts off your round, the retail Super Select will do the job just as well.

Final Verdict

The Scotty Cameron Circle T GoLo 6.5 is a stunning piece of engineering that holds its value better than almost any other club in the bag. It is a great choice if you view it as both a tool and an investment, but don't expect the "Circle T" stamp to make the ball go in the hole for you.
